message.content:
The official Cypress **Best Practices** guide covers the items you listed, but it phrases some of them as concrete patterns rather than exactly as you wrote them. Below is a source-grounded enumeration of the relevant **best practices** and **anti-patterns** for end-to-end testing from Cypress’s official docs, with the reason and the docs URL for each.[1]

| Practice / anti-pattern | Reason given by Cypress | Docs.cypress.io URL |
|---|---|---|
| **Best practice:** Use `data-*` attributes to select elements instead of brittle CSS selectors or IDs | Cypress says selectors should be isolated from CSS or JavaScript changes so tests do not break when styling or structure changes.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Selecting-Elements |
| **Anti-pattern:** Use highly brittle selectors that are subject to change | Brittle selectors make tests fragile when the app UI changes.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Selecting-Elements |
| **Anti-pattern:** Use arbitrary `cy.wait(time)` delays | Fixed waits slow tests down and do not express what condition the test is actually waiting for.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Unnecessary-Waiting |
| **Best practice:** Use route aliases or assertions to wait for explicit conditions instead of time-based waits | Cypress recommends waiting on a known network alias or an assertion so the test proceeds only when the app is ready.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Unnecessary-Waiting |
| **Best practice:** Keep tests independent so they can run alone and still pass | Independent tests are easier to maintain and do not create order dependency or hidden coupling.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Having-tests-rely-on-the-state-of-previous-tests |
| **Anti-pattern:** Make tests rely on the state of previous tests | Coupling tests together makes failures cascade and makes the suite non-deterministic.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Having-tests-rely-on-the-state-of-previous-tests |
| **Best practice:** Control application state programmatically instead of using the UI for setup | Cypress recommends shortcuts such as programmatic login or API-driven setup to avoid slow, brittle UI setup flows.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Organizing-Tests-Logging-In-Controlling-State |
| **Anti-pattern:** Use the UI to log in or otherwise set up state when a shortcut exists | UI setup adds unnecessary steps and increases test brittleness.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Organizing-Tests-Logging-In-Controlling-State |
| **Anti-pattern:** Assign return values from Cypress commands with `const`, `let`, or `var` | Cypress commands are asynchronous, so direct assignment does not work reliably.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Assigning-Return-Values |
| **Best practice:** Use closures or aliases instead of assigning command return values | Cypress recommends `.then()` closures or `.as()` aliases to access yielded values later.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Assigning-Return-Values |
| **Best practice:** Let a test contain multiple assertions when needed; avoid making a single test do too much by splitting by behavior rather than forcing “tiny” unit-style tests | Cypress explicitly rejects the “single assertion only” mindset and recommends writing meaningful end-to-end tests with multiple assertions when appropriate.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Creating-tiny-tests-with-a-single-assertion |
| **Anti-pattern:** Act like you are writing unit tests | Cypress says this leads to overly small tests that do not reflect real end-to-end user flows.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Creating-tiny-tests-with-a-single-assertion |
| **Best practice:** Set a `baseUrl` in configuration and use relative `cy.visit()` URLs | Cypress recommends defining `baseUrl` centrally rather than hard-coding full URLs in tests.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Setting-a-global-baseUrl |
| **Anti-pattern:** Use `cy.visit()` without setting a `baseUrl` | Hard-coded navigation reduces maintainability and makes environment changes harder.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Setting-a-global-baseUrl |
| **Anti-pattern:** Start a web server from inside Cypress tests with `cy.exec()` or `cy.task()` | Cypress recommends starting the server before the test run, not as part of the test itself.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Web-Servers |
| **Best practice:** Start the web server prior to running Cypress | This keeps the test runner focused on testing rather than process orchestration.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Web-Servers |
| **Anti-pattern:** Clean up state in `after` or `afterEach` hooks | Cypress recommends cleaning up *before* tests run so each test starts from a known state.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Using-after-or-afterEach-hooks |
| **Best practice:** Clean up state before tests run | Pre-test cleanup produces a consistent, isolated starting point for each test.[1] | docs.cypress.io/guides/references/best-practices#Using-after-or-afterEach-hooks |

A small nuance: Cypress’s official text is most explicit about **`data-*` attributes** and **independent tests**; it does not use your exact wording “avoid a single test doing too much” as a standalone headline, but it does directly discourage “tiny tests with a single assertion,” which is the opposite extreme.[1] Similarly, Cypress’s “control state programmatically” guidance is presented under “Organizing Tests, Logging In, Controlling State,” with the concrete example of avoiding UI login and taking shortcuts where possible.[1]

If you want, I can turn this into a stricter audit checklist with one row per exact Cypress doc subsection and direct quote fragments under 50 words.
